nodes.name: HawkesProcessSimulator
nodes.description: Simulates a univariate Hawkes self-exciting point process over the interval [0, T] using Ogata's thinning algorithm. Given baseline intensity mu, excitation amplitude alpha, and exponential decay rate beta, draws stochastic event times whose conditional intensity is λ(t) = μ + Σᵢ α·exp(−β(t−tᵢ)) for all past events tᵢ < t. Returns the full realisation as an array of arrival times.

inputs.name: alpha
inputs.type_desc: float — excitation amplitude, controls jump size at each event

inputs.name: beta
inputs.type_desc: float — exponential decay rate of self-excitement kernel

inputs.name: T
inputs.type_desc: float — simulation horizon, events drawn on (0, T]

outputs.name: event_times
outputs.type_desc: array[float] — ordered arrival times of simulated events in (0, T]
outputs.constraints: monotonically increasing, all values in (0, T]

nodes.type_signature: (mu: float — baseline (background) intensity, μ > 0, alpha: float — excitation amplitude, controls jump size at each event, beta: float — exponential decay rate of self-excitement kernel, T: float — simulation horizon, events drawn on (0, T]) -> array[float] — ordered arrival times of simulated events in (0, T]
nodes.conceptual_summary: SelfExcitingStochasticArrivalSampler. Generates a realization of a temporally ordered set of scalar arrival times over a bounded interval by sampling from a non-stationary stochastic point process whose instantaneous rate is a superposition of a constant baseline intensity and a sum of exponentially decaying excitation kernels triggered by each prior arrival — a feedback mechanism that makes the process self-reinforcing. The underlying rate function is integrated via an acceptance-rejection (thinning) step against a tractable upper-bounding envelope, ensuring exact simulation without discretization error. The result is a monotonically ordered array of continuous scalar timestamps whose inter-arrival statistics are neither independent nor identically distributed, but are governed by the full path history.. Applications: Seismology / Geophysics: Modeling aftershock sequences following a mainshock rupture, where each seismic event transiently elevates the probability of subsequent ruptures — the decay kernel captures the empirical Omori-Utsu power-law approximated by exponential relaxation, enabling synthetic catalog generation for hazard assessment., Computational Neuroscience: Simulating spike train bursting in recurrently coupled neural populations, where each action potential raises the short-term excitability of the same or downstream neuron before synaptic depression restores baseline firing rate — enabling synthetic electrophysiology data for testing spike-sorting and connectivity-inference algorithms., Epidemiology / Contagion Modeling: Generating synthetic transmission event timelines for a disease or information cascade in which each infection or repost event temporarily amplifies the local transmission rate before immunity, removal, or attention decay suppresses it — useful for calibrating branching-process reproductive number estimators., Materials Science / Reliability Engineering: Simulating cascading micro-fracture or dislocation avalanche sequences in stressed crystalline lattices, where each structural failure event locally concentrates stress and elevates the instantaneous rate of neighboring failures, modeling the temporal clustering observed in acoustic emission signals during fatigue testing.
